content.text: Resolving an incident closes the technical chapter but opens a separate customer trust chapter. Customers who experienced a disruption want confirmation that it is over, an honest explanation of what happened, and evidence that the team is preventing recurrence. Post-incident follow-up is the structured process for delivering those three things in the right order, through the right channels, on the right timelines.

A poorly executed follow-up — late, vague, or missing entirely — erodes more trust than the incident itself. A well-executed follow-up can actually strengthen the customer relationship by demonstrating transparency and organisational competence.

attrs.alt: Post-incident follow-up timeline showing five milestones: T=0 resolution, +30 min resolution email, +2h internal hot-wash, +24h preliminary postmortem draft, +5 days public incident report, +30 days prevention action review. Enterprise direct outreach and GDPR 72h windows annotated above.

content.text: The moment an incident is marked RESOLVED on the status page, the follow-up clock starts. Two actions are required within the first two hours.

For P0 incidents, a resolution email is sent to all affected customers within 30 minutes of the RESOLVED status post. The email confirms the service is restored, states the total duration, and commits to a public incident report date.

Subject: [RESOLVED] Claude API Incident — {incident_date}

The Claude API degradation that began at {start_time_utc} has been resolved
as of {resolved_time_utc}. Total duration: {duration}.

All API endpoints are operating normally.

We will publish a full incident report by {report_deadline_date}.

We apologise for the impact on your work. If you have questions in the
meantime, contact support with incident ID {incident_id}.
For P1 incidents, a resolution email goes to Enterprise customers only. For P2 and P3, the status page RESOLVED post is sufficient — no email is sent.

Within two hours of resolution, the IC convenes a brief (30-minute maximum) hot-wash with the on-call engineer, Comms Lead, and Tech Lead. The hot-wash is not a postmortem — it captures immediate observations while they are fresh, before people rotate off-call.
What was the first customer-visible symptom?
What was the hardest part of the response?
Hot-wash notes are saved to the incident Notion page within 24 hours. They seed the preliminary postmortem draft.

Within 24 hours of resolution, the on-call engineer produces a preliminary postmortem draft. This is an internal document, not a customer document. Its purpose is to capture timeline facts and early hypotheses before context is lost.

Enterprise customers with SLA agreements — typically Tier 1 and Tier 2 accounts — receive a personalised follow-up call or meeting request within 24 hours of a P0. The Comms Lead coordinates this with the Account Executive team; it is not the on-call engineer's responsibility.

The written summary for enterprise accounts is more detailed than the public incident report and includes the customer-specific impact scope pulled from API logs. It does not include internal postmortem findings or root cause hypotheses that are still unconfirmed.

If the incident triggered an SLA breach, the credit calculation must be initiated before the incident record is closed. The Comms Lead flags the incident for SLA review; the Finance team calculates the credit based on the verified downtime duration.
Phase 3: Public Incident Report (5 Business Days)

Within five business days of P0 or P1 resolution, a public-facing incident report is published to the Anthropic blog and linked from the status page. This document is customer-appropriate — it describes what happened and what was done without exposing internal postmortem findings, system architecture details, or unresolved hypotheses.
Summary (2–3 sentences): what happened, when, and that it is resolved
Timeline (UTC): key events from first alert to resolution
Customer impact: specific error rate, affected scope, duration
Root cause: factual, confirmed description — no speculation
What we did: containment and fix steps
What we are doing: concrete preventive actions with target dates

The root cause section must state confirmed facts only. If investigation is still ongoing at the 5-day mark, write: 'Root cause investigation is ongoing. We will update this report when confirmed.' Do not publish a speculative root cause to meet the deadline.
Postmortem root cause confirmed by engineering
Draft reviewed by IC for accuracy
Draft reviewed by Legal for privacy incidents
Action items have owners and dates
No internal tool names or staff names in public text
Status page RESOLVED post updated with link to report
Enterprise customers notified of report publication

Action items generated in the postmortem are reviewed at the 30-day mark to assess completion and effectiveness. This review is separate from the immediate postmortem meeting — it closes the learning loop and prevents postmortem action items from being quietly dropped.

Every postmortem action item must have: a unique ID, a named owner, a concrete deliverable, and a target date. Vague action items — 'improve monitoring' without specifying which metric or alert — are rejected at the postmortem review meeting.
Add P95 latency alert on /v1/messages with threshold 2.5s
Alert firing in production, documented in runbook
Review each action item: complete, in-progress, or blocked
For blocked items: identify blocker, reassign or extend with justification
For completed items: verify effectiveness — did the action actually close the risk?
Identify any new risks discovered during implementation
Update the postmortem with action item outcomes
If a prevention measure cannot be completed: document why and what compensating control exists
The 30-day review is owned by the Engineering Manager of the team that experienced the incident, not the IC. The IC's responsibility ends when the public incident report is published.

For P0 incidents, the Comms Lead sends a brief customer satisfaction survey 7 days after resolution. The survey has a single question: 'How satisfied were you with Anthropic's communication during the incident?' (scale 1–5) plus an optional free-text field. Results are reviewed in the 30-day retrospective.

For incidents involving personal data, the follow-up timeline is governed by GDPR Article 33 (supervisory authority notification) and Article 34 (data subject notification), not the standard 5-day report deadline.
Nature of breach, categories of data, approximate number of records, likely consequences, measures taken
As soon as further info available
Completed information that was absent from initial filing
Without undue delay if high risk
Nature of breach in plain language, contact details of DPO, likely consequences, measures taken

The 72-hour Article 33 clock starts at the moment the organisation 'becomes aware' of the breach — which is the point a reasonable assessment indicates a breach has likely occurred, not when the investigation is complete. Early notification with incomplete information is required; updates can be filed as more details become available.
Publishing the incident report before root cause is confirmed — speculation erodes trust more than delay
Missing the 5-day deadline without notifying affected customers that the report is delayed and giving a new date
Action items without owners — they will not be completed
Hot-wash skipped because the team is tired — context loss at this stage makes postmortem quality significantly worse
Enterprise customers notified by automated email instead of a personal outreach from their Account Executive after a P0
CSAT survey not sent — the 7-day window closes quickly and the opportunity for structured feedback is lost
30-day review cancelled or postponed indefinitely — the most common reason postmortem action items are never completed

Post-incident customer follow-up operates across four phases: immediate (0–2h), short-term (2h–5d), medium-term (5d public report), and long-term (30d prevention review). Each phase has mandatory deliverables, owners, and deadlines that do not change based on team capacity.
Resolution email within 30 minutes for P0 (30-minute window is firm)
Hot-wash within 2 hours to capture context before it is lost
Enterprise direct outreach within 24 hours for P0 Tier 1 accounts
SLA credit calculation initiated before incident record is closed
Public incident report within 5 business days — only if root cause is confirmed; delay and communicate if not
GDPR Article 33 notification within 72 hours for any data incident — preliminary filing is acceptable
30-day prevention action review closes the learning loop
